title: Authentication, Authorization, RBAC, Service Accounts, Tokens, and Impersonation · Object and Event Forensics
summary: An incident responder inherits a plausible but defective least-privilege identity and authorization policy; preserve status and events, isolate the first causal fault in authentication, authorization, rbac, service accounts, tokens, and impersonation, and retain a regression check. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in least-privilege identity and authorization policy and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit th…
objectives: Trace TLS identity, authentication, user and group information, authorization, admission, validation, persistence, and audit for one request.
Separate external human users and groups, ServiceAccounts, node identities, workload identity federation, image pull identity, and bootstrap credentials.
Construct namespace-scoped Roles and RoleBindings or justified ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings from verbs, resources, names, non-resource URLs, and subjects.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug K8s Authn Authz Admission Chain
steps.instruction:

Derive subject, verb, resource, namespace, token audience, impersonation, and least-privilege proof. Competency probe: Trace TLS identity, authentication, user and group information, authorization, admission, validation, persistence, and audit for one request.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair · Debug K8s Token Audience Rotation Review
steps.instruction:
Append the Kubernetes evidence item headed "  # Evidence: k8s-token-audience-rotation-review-106v9z". Tailor its rbac-policy.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic API or manifest review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run kubectl, Helm, kubeadm, and cluster commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Implement the cause-level repair · Debug K8s Token Audience Rotation Review.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns K8s Token Audience Rotation Review into observable performance evidence. For Implement the cause-level repair · Debug K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, keep K8s Token Audience Rotation Review connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-token-audience-rotation-review-106v9z
  - competency: k8s-token-audience-rotation-review
    artifact: rbac-policy.yaml
    decision: token audience rotation review
    environment: remote context with short-lived federated workload identity
    fault-injection: change a selector so one canary endpoint overlaps production
    retained-proof: scheduler event eligible domains skew and placement result
    case-key: 106v9z
    change: |
      serviceAccount: intake/status-api
      automountServiceAccountToken: false
      token: projected-audience-intake-api
      role: get-configmap-status-only
      binding: namespace-intake
      review: auth-can-i-and-escalation-map
    verify: kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:intake:status-api -n intake
    failure: Reject broad ClusterRoleBinding, long-lived static tokens, wildcard verbs, secret reads, bind or escalate paths, or workload creation as harmless.
    repair: Separate human and workload identity, scope verbs and resources, use bound audience tokens, test authorization, and review escalation periodically.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.

hints: For Publish the incident record · Debug K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, name the context, namespace, API identity, desired state, observed status, controller, policy, and failure-domain boundary, K8s Token Audience Rotation Review precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Publish the incident record · Debug K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, trace this concrete input and predict admission, defaulting, scheduling, reconciliation, status, traffic, storage, policy, disruption, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A debug workload uses the default ServiceAccount, automounts a broad token, and receives wildcard cluster-admin through a group binding. Derive subject, verb, resource, namespace, token audience, impersonation, and least-privilege proof. Competency probe: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.

For Publish the incident record · Debug K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, challenge the documented misconception with object, condition, event, policy, failure, repair, rollback, and transfer evidence: A projected service account token is valid forever and for every service that trusts the cluster issuer.

checks.description: The evidence cycle runs from problem framing through changed-case validation.
checks.failureMessage: Frame the decision before modeling, and validate after obtaining a result.
